coredata.dc:title: Application of carbon dots in nucleolus imaging to distinguish cancerous cells from normal cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Boye
Duan, Qianqian
Zhao, Haichao
Zhang, Yixia
Li, Xiaoning
Xi, Yanfeng
Wu, Zhifang
Guo, Li
Li, Pengcui
Sang, Shengbo
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent molecular probes are very critical for detecting the precise location of lesions in fundamental research and clinical applications. Herein, we report a green-emitting carbon dots (m-CDs) with excellent properties including good photostability, high cellular uptake efficiency, low cytotoxicity, and good counterstain compatibility. Digest tests of ribonuclease indicate the significantly higher affinity of m-CDs for nucleolus RNA in both fixed and living cells. Notably, visualizing nucleoli in cells by using m-CDs can accurately achieve its number and morphology information to distinguish cancerous cells from normal cells. In addition, m-CDs can also be used to monitor the nucleolar dynamics during apoptosis of malignant cells induced by doxorubicin (DOX). The m-CDs show great potential as a nucleolus probe, and promising applications in cancerous cells screening and therapeutic efficacy evaluation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Cell imaging
Nucleolus

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Long-term monitoring nucleolar dynamics during cell apoptosis
Living cell imaging property of the m-CDs
The mechanism of selective staining of nucleolus by m-CDs
Use m-CDs to distinguish normal cells and cancerous cells
Long-term monitoring of cellular nucleolar dynamics in real-time with m-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis of m-CDs and the selective staining of nucleolus.

(a) TEM image of m-CDs (inset: Particle size distribution of m-CDs). (b) FTIR spectra of m-CDs. (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of m-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (d) Photostability comparison of Hoechst 33342 and m-CDs.

(a) Bright field image of SMMC-7721 cells. (b) Fluorescence images of SMMC-7721 cells after incubated with medium containing m-CDs for 2 h under excitation at 488 nm. (c) Blue fluorescence image of nucleus stained with Hoechst 33342 and excited at 365 nm. (d) The merged image of (a) and (b). (e) The merged image of (b) and (c). (f) Relative fluorescence intensity profiles along the red line shown in panels (b) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Fluorescence images of SMMC-7721 cells stained by m-CDs, SYTO RNASelect or Hoechst 33342 in DNase and RNase digestion experiments.
(a) Fluorescence images of SMMC-7721 and LO2 cell lines stained by m-CDs. (b) Frequency distributions of the number of nucleoli per cell for SMMC-7721 and LO2 cell lines.
(a) Statistics of average number of nucleoli in different cell types. (b) Observation of enlarged nucleolus morphology.
Imaging of nucleolus in SMMC-7721 cells with m-CDs after apoptosis-inducing strategies treatment.

head.ce:title: Application of carbon dots in nucleolus imaging to distinguish cancerous cells from normal cells

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Orthopedics, Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent molecular probes are very critical for detecting the precise location of lesions in fundamental research and clinical applications. Herein, we report a green-emitting carbon dots (m-CDs) with excellent properties including good photostability, high cellular uptake efficiency, low cytotoxicity, and good counterstain compatibility. Digest tests of ribonuclease indicate the significantly higher affinity of m-CDs for nucleolus RNA in both fixed and living cells. Notably, visualizing nucleoli in cells by using m-CDs can accurately achieve its number and morphology information to distinguish cancerous cells from normal cells. In addition, m-CDs can also be used to monitor the nucleolar dynamics during apoptosis of malignant cells induced by doxorubicin (DOX). The m-CDs show great potential as a nucleolus probe, and promising applications in cancerous cells screening and therapeutic efficacy evaluation.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent probe has broad application prospects in clinical procedures from cell and histopathological diagnosis to tumor localization and image-guided surgery [1–5]. The specific local imaging of subcellular organelles (such as nucleoli and mitochondria) is of great significance for in-depth understanding of biological processes and pathways for early disease diagnosis and biomedical drug development [6–10]. Thus, achieving effective organelle selection and the accurate subcellular localization of fluorescent probes are the main considerations for designing more reasonable chemical modification [11–15].

At present, in order to use carbon dots (CDs) more effectively, widely and practically, effective subcellular selection and screening are still required. But there is still a lack of a deep understanding of more rational design of the chemical modifications necessary for achieving this goal. RNA is related to varieties of functions in biological systems spanning from transcriptional regulation to essential catalytic roles [16–21]. The dynamics of ribosomal RNA (rRNA) that assemble in the nucleolus are involved in a wide range of physiological activities such as cell mitosis, proliferation, stress response [22,23]. Therefore, nucleoli are important subnuclear structures inside cells. Additionally, disruption of the nucleolus function can induce a series of pathogenic conditions triggered by errors in ribosome biogenesis. For example, the enlargement of nucleolus or number abnormality usually indicates a specific type of cancer and other human diseases [24–27]. Therefore, the diagnostic biomarkers of nucleolus such as its size, shape and number, have received considerable attention in the pathological detection of malignant lesions. Monitoring the status and changes of nucleolus is significant for studying its biological behavior, function and better understanding of diseases related to nucleolus.

Fluorescence imaging technology is a very powerful tool for monitoring nucleolus biological processes. Designing and constructing nucleolar targeting probes to meet the needs of cell imaging is currently a hot research topic. However, there has been only one commercially available nucleolar probe, SYTO RNASelect, which emits green fluorescence and only stains RNA in the fixed cells. Additionally, some other methods for imaging RNA have been developed such as novel fluorescent gold nanoclusters [28], carbogenic small molecular complex (SMC) [29], copper/carbon quantum dot-crosslinked nanosheets (CuCD NSs) [30], and organic small molecules (OSMs) [31]. Despite these progresses in the investigations of cellular RNA dynamics, challenges such as specificity, targeting ability, long-term imaging capability, and strict storage conditions, have restricted their widespread application in real-time live-cell imaging. These challenges, especially the requirements for long-term imaging under continuous irradiation, are important in studying the dynamics of RNA in cellular processes.

The ideal probe should be specifically enriched in the target area to accurately locate with minimal side effects [32–38]. Recently, carbon nanomaterials such as CDs, are widely regarded as advantageous in terms of material toxicity [39–43]. But there is still a key problem: how to make reasonable modifications to assure the stable and target stain of cytoplasm or nucleolus. It has been reported that RNA-Selective dyes generally have the common characteristics of aromatic rings, nitrogen-containing heterocycles and amines [44–47]. Therefore, the selection of suitable carbon and nitrogen sources has become a key element.

As a proof of concept, three types of CDs (o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs) were separately synthesised and then used to label different cell types (Figure S1). The m-CDs were distributed throughout the cytoplasm and mainly concentrated in the nucleoli, while o-CDs and p-CDs were mainly concentrated in the perinuclear region and failed to target nucleoli obviously. The m-CDs showed a strong affinity to rRNA, and could be used to allow visualization of nucleoli in cells for clear statistics (Scheme 1 ). The number of nucleoli in cancerous cells and normal cells could be counted by imaging nucleoli with m-CDs to clearly observe the difference between nucleoli. At the same time, nucleolus tracking was realized by m-CDs under the action of drugs, thereby monitoring the process of cell apoptosis. Monitoring the state of cells during the process of apoptosis shows the potential application in nucleolus research and real-time monitoring.

L-cysteine, o-phenylenediamine (o-PD), m-phenylenediamine (m-PD), or p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). LO2, GES-1, HEM-d, Fibroblasts (FB), SMMC-7721, MGC-803, Mel-RM, H7, HeLa and SKOV3 cells were provided by the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 medium, Dulbecco's Modification of Eagle's Medium (DMEM) medium and other reagents used for cell culture were purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water purified by a Millipore Milli-Q system (Billerica, MA, USA) was used throughout the experiments.

The morphological characteristics of CDs were measured by a high-resolution TEM (JEOL-2100 F, Tokyo, Japan). The surface functional groups of the samples were detected by fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR; Bruker OPTIK GmbH Tensor 27, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Scientifific ESCALAB 250Xi, America). The absorption spectra were obtained from a UV–vis spectrophotometer in the wavelength range 190 nm–1200 nm (METASH UV8000, Shanghai, China). Fluorescence spectra were evaluated using a FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Livingston, UK). The OD values of MTT assay for cytotoxicity test were measured using a microplate reader (Multiskan Go, Thermo Scientifc, MA, USA). All images of cells were observed by a ﬂuorescence microscope (Leica DM3000, Wetzlar, Germany).

800 mg of l-cysteine and 500 mg of o-PD, m-PD, or p-PD were dissolved in 30 mL of double distilled water. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL teﬂon lined autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 8 h. The resulting mixture was then removed solvents and further dried under vacuum. Finally, three kinds of pure CDs (o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs) were obtained and stored at 4 °C for further use.
All cells (SMMC-7721, LO2, MGC-803, GES-1, Mel-RM, HEM-d, FB, H7, HeLa and SKOV3 cells) were cultured with RPMI 1640 or DMEM with 10 % FBS in an air-5 % CO2 incubator at 37 °C.

Cell viability was determined by the MTT method. Brieﬂy, cells were seeded in 96-well plates with an initial density of 1 × 104/well. After the cells reached 80 % subconfluency, the culture media was replaced with fresh media and treated with m-CDs for 24 h and 48 h, respectively. After incubation, 10 μL of MTT solution was then added to each well, and incubated at 37 °C for a further 4 h. Thereafter, the medium was discarded and followed by the addition of 100 μL of DMSO to each well to dissolve the violet formazan crystals. Lastly, the absorbance of each well was read immediately at 450 nm using a microplate reader.

All cells were seeded on glass slides in 24-well plates. After the cells reached 80 % subconﬂuency, cells were treated with the m-CDs for different times (5 min, 15 min, 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 8 h, and 12 h). After incubation, cells were washed thoroughly by PBS for three times to remove extracellular m-CDs, fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde, and then washed again in PBS. Typically, the cell samples were incubated for 30 min with m-CDs before imaging by fluorescence microscopy. After the cells were fixed, the cells were treated with Hoechst 33342 (Sigma, MO, USA). Cellular fluorescent images of m-CDs were observed under ﬂuorescence microscopy with a long-wavelength pass channel (>515 nm) and excitation channel (450 nm–490 nm). Finally, the fluorescence intensity was calculated by Image-Pro Plus 5.1.

Counterstaining experiments using m-CDs and Hoechst 33342: Cells were incubated with m-CDs in medium at 37 °C for 30 min. After washing twice with PBS, the same sample was stained with Hoechst 33342 for 30 min, and then imaged by a fluorescence microscope.
The photostability test was carried out by continuously irradiating m-CDs and Hoechst 33342-stained cells with a mercury lamp, and observed under a fluorescence microscope.

SMMC-7721 cells were first fixed in prechilled methanol (−20 °C) for 1 min and then permeabilized with 1% Triton X-100 for 2 min at room temperature. After washed twice with PBS, cells were treated with 50 μg/mL DNase, 100 μg/mL DNase-free RNase, and PBS solution (control experiment) for 3 h at 37 °C in 5 % CO2, respectively. Cells were subsequently stained with m-CDs (5 μg/mL), Hoechst 33342 (5 μg/mL) or SYTO RNASelect (5 μM) solution for 30 min. After washing with PBS, fluorescence images were obtained using ﬂuorescence microscopy with equal exposure time for all samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-term monitoring nucleolar dynamics during cell apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, SMMC-7721 cells were incubated with m-CDs for 30 min and then separately treated with 10 μg/mL doxorubicin (DOX) for different time (0 h, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h, 12 h, and 24 h). The cells were then observed by a fluorescence microscopy.

As the characters of m-CDs shown in Fig. 1 a, the m-CDs were well-dispersed with spherical shape, and had a uniform size of 3−4 nm, which was close to the expected values. Most importantly, these values fell below the dominating channel of nuclear pore complexes (∼5.2 nm in diameter), which was crucial for nanomaterials to migrate through the nuclear envelope and reach the nucleolar regions [28]. In order to determine surface functional groups composition of m-CDs, we conducted FTIR spectroscopy. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1b), the peak at 3336 cm−1 corresponded to NH and OH stretching vibrations. The peak at 1697 cm−1 indicated the presence of CO stretching vibration. The peaks centered at 2927, 1586, 1388, 1197 cm-1 and 1003 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of CH, CC, CN, CO and CS, respectively [48–50]. According to the FTIR spectrum, the surface of m-CDs was rich in NH2 and COOH groups. Moreover, the ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum exhibited two absorption peaks centered at 294 nm and 420 nm, revealing the presence of π-π* transition and the n-π* transition (aromatic CC and CN) from the surface states of the m-CDs, respectively.51 The fluorescent emission peaks were almost fixed around 530 nm and independent of excitation wavelength (Fig. 1c).

We further provided the UV–vis absorption, FTIR, and XPS spectra for further studying the chemical properties of the three CDs (Figure S2-S5). Results showed that only the nitrogen content of the three CDs appeared to be distinctly different. According to incomplete survey, N-doped CDs are efficient for internalization and targeting of the nucleus [30,52,53]. One of the important reasons why these CDs have different characteristics is based on their different nitrogen content [42,51,54,55]. These variations may be partly responsible for the different nucleolar targeting abilities of the three CDs. However, the more in-depth study was needed to explore the mechanism of the nucleolar targeting in the future. Moreover, the fluorescence stability of m-CDs under different conditions: pH values, UV irradiation time, storage time, and different batches were satisfactory (Figs. S6 and S7).

Additionally, photostability of fluorescent probes is critical in biological imaging. Hence, we further studied the photostability characteristics of m-CDs in SMMC-7721 cells. As depicted in Figs. 1d and S8, there was still strong fluorescence intensity in m-CDs-treated cells after irradiation for 7 min with a mercury lamp, whereas almost no fluorescence was observed in Hoechst 33342-treated cells under the same condition. Specifically, the fluorescence signal of Hoechst 33342 dropped to ∼0 after irradiation for 7 min because of severe photobleaching, whereas that of the m-CDs decreased to only ∼82 % of its original intensity after irradiation. From the above comparison data, the m-CDs exhibited much superior photostability than Hoechst 33342.

Figure S9 showed fluorescence images of SMMC-7721 cells incubated with m-CDs for different times. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity in the cells gradually enhanced with increasing incubation time. m-CDs could be rapidly internalized by SMMC-7721 cells only for 5 min, showing its cellular high internalization efficiency. Notably, comparing with the commonly reported a few hours for effective internalization and visualization of CDs [55–57], m-CDs revealed significant advantage for rapid bio-imaging, which could be comparable with the commercial dyes. For example, the SYTO RNASelect fluorescent cell stain takes about 20 min [22]. Considering the safety of potential biomedical applications, the biocompatibility of m-CDs was evaluated by the MTT assays on SMMC-7721 and LO2 cell lines (Figure S10). Results showed that the cell viability was as high as 84 % even at a high concentration (20 μg/mL) for 24 h and 48 h, which indicated that the m-CDs had no significant cytotoxic effect on SMMC-7721 and LO2 cells. The excellent fluorescence characteristics, good photostability, low toxicity, and rapid staining ability of m-CDs make it an ideal choice for biological imaging applications. Therefore, we studied the cell imaging ability of m-CDs using SMMC-7721 cells as a model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Living cell imaging property of the m-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the staining sites in the cells, we counterstained the cells with m-CDs and nuclear dye Hoechst 33342, respectively. Fig. 2 b showed that m-CDs could penetrate into cells and display bright green fluorescence with 488 nm laser irradiation. As depicted in Fig. 2e, the stained parts of m-CDs and Hoechst 33342 could overlap well, indicating that m-CDs have entered the nucleus. Similar phenomena have been observed in other cells. Analysis of the fluorescence intensity distribution in the cells with Image-Pro Plus 5.1 further showed that the fluorescence intensity in the nucleus was not evenly distributed, but scattered in some circular areas (Fig. 2f). Thus we further speculated that the staining sites by m-CDs in cells might be nucleoli.

It is significant to use multiple probes to stain multiple targets simultaneously for observing the connection between different organelles during cell imaging. According to Fig. 2e, m-CDs and Hoechst 33342 could keep their own labeling pattern and showed no interference effects between each other in bio-imaging. Excellent counterstaining compatibility of m-CDs makes it to have great potential for multi-staining imaging. Since the nucleolus is an RNA-rich region, 19 we speculated that m-CDs have a higher affinity for RNA than DNA and other substances in the cytoplasm. In order to further confirm the hypothesis that m-CDs could stain nucleoli, we conducted digestion experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of selective staining of nucleolus by m-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since m-CDs and SYTO RNASelect show very similar luminescent color, the counterstaining is ineffective to confirm RNA targeting. To verify our conjecture, RNase and DNase digestion experiments were conducted to test the selectivity of m-CDs in cell nucleus.

As depicted in Fig. 3 a, both the luminescent nucleolus and cytoplasm were observed in control cells. After treatment with DNase, the luminescence signal in the nucleolus and cytoplasm was not significantly lost, and the fluorescence distribution did not change significantly (Fig. 3b). However, after treatment with RNase, the fluorescence intensity of m-CDs in cytoplasm and nucleolus was significantly reduced, the characteristics of nucleolus disappeared, and the fluorescence distribution was also significantly different (Fig. 3c). About 30 % of the original maximum fluorescence value remained after RNase digestion (Fig. 3a and c), compared to about 87 % after DNase digestion (Fig. 3a and b). SYTO RNASelect showed similar results in digestion experiment. Specifically, about 50 % of the original maximum fluorescence value remained after RNase treatment (Fig. 3d and f) and about 80 % remaining after DNase digestion (Fig. 3d and e).

Because Hoechst 33342 staining dye is a fluorescent stain for labeling DNA rather than RNA, only the digestion treatment with DNase has a significant effect on Hoechst staining results (Fig. 3g–i). Digestion results which similar to SYTO RNASelect further confirmed the selective binding of m-CDs to rRNA in nucleoli. Compared with commercial dyes, the response to changes in fluorescence intensity during digestion experiments in m-CDs-treated cells is more sensitive, and contrast before and after the RNase digestion is stronger. Additionally, the m-CDs can stain both live and dead cells (Figure S11). According to the above experimental results, the m-CDs have the potential to be used as a nucleolus stain. Based on this, the following two applications in nucleolar imaging were carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Use m-CDs to distinguish normal cells and cancerous cells
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the different nuclear features between cancerous and normal cells, SMMC-7721 and LO2 cells were then treated with m-CDs. The average number of nucleoli per cell was 3.92 and 1.69 corresponding to SMMC-7721 and LO2 cells, respectively (Fig. 4 b). The significant difference in normal cells and cancerous cells was further demonstrated by the statistical analysis result showed in Fig. 4. To further study the dependence of nuclear imaging feature on cell lines, various normal cells (GES-1, FB and HEM-d) and cancerous cells (MGC-803, Mel-RM, H7, HeLa and SKOV3) were incubated with m-CDs (Figure S12-S14). As expected, the nucleoli in all of these cell types were clearly displayed in green channel, which extensively verified the capability of nuclear staining.

Specifically, the different number of nucleoli in different types of cells were clearly presented. The number of nucleoli in most cancerous cells was more than 3, and the number was smaller than 2 in these normal cells (Fig. 5 a). In addition, most nucleoli were found irregularly shaped and featureless in cancerous cells, but more uniform and rounder in normal cells (Fig. 5b). These results strongly suggest that m-CDs can be used as a fluorescent probe to screen for the cancerous cells and have the potential for early cancer diagnosis in the cellular level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-term monitoring of cellular nucleolar dynamics in real-time with m-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A potential wide application in nucleolar research is the study of the mechanism of drug action and the evaluation of the associated therapeutic progress. The nucleolus-targeting imaging capability of m-CDs makes it possible to monitor real-time changes in nucleoli under the influence of drugs. The nucleolar dynamics in SMMC-7721 cells with the presence of apoptosis inducers was used to investigate the real-time imaging capability of m-CDs. DOX is one of the most excellent anthracycline anti-tumor drugs. It is a cell cycle non-specific drug, which can inhibit the synthesis of DNA and RNA. Therefore, we established a model of cell apoptosis induced by DOX, and realized the observing the apoptosis process in real-time.

As shown in Fig. 6 , m-CDs can realize the real-time visualization of the state of the nucleoli using fluorescence tracing. Specifically, after 6 h of DOX treatment, the nucleoli started to shrink and became smaller. The nucleoli became blurred and scattered after 12 h of DOX treatment. Then, the morphology of these cells changed from spindle shape to sphere shape, and then gradually shrank after 24 h of DOX treatment. These results showed that m-CDs could not only be used to observe the nucleolus, but also show changes in the contour shapes of the entire cells. The inhibitory effect of DOX on SMMC-7721 cells was obviously observed through these changes. The identification of DOX-induced cell responses revealed a transition stage from the living state to the death of the cells, thus providing a new sensitive mode of studying relevant changes in cells and nucleoli. Therefore, it is expected that m-CDs can be used to visually track cell nucleoli.

In summary, a specific nucleolus probe based on m-CDs was developed. The m-CDs have many unique functions, including ultra-small size, good photostability, high cellular uptake efficiency, low cytotoxicity, and good counterstain compatibility. The m-CDs can specifically and stably display the nucleoli in various types of cells, thereby realizing the distinction between cancerous cells and normal cells through the characteristics of nucleoli. Additionally, the dynamic changes of nucleoli in the cells by pretreatment with DOX can be tracked, thereby providing a method for real-time monitoring of cell apoptosis and evaluating the efficacy of drugs. The significant features of targeted imaging nucleoli and cancerous cells recognition by m-CDs make it to be an excellent fluorescent probe for various biomedical applications.

Boye Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Qianqian Duan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Haichao Zhao: Resources, Formal analysis. Yixia Zhang: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Xiaoning Li: Formal analysis, Methodology. Yanfeng Xi: Methodology, Resources. Zhifang Wu: Resources, Conceptualization. Li Guo: Resources, Project administration. Pengcui Li: Resources, Formal analysis. Shengbo Sang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51975400, 51622507, 51505324), Basic Research Program of Shanxi for Youths (201701D221111), Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (201802036).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.129156.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel immunosensor for squamous cell carcinoma antigen determination based on CdTe@Carbon dots nanocomposite electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Luo, X. Yang, Y. Wan, C. Liu, A novel immunosensor for squamous cell carcinoma antigen determination based on CdTe@Carbon dots nanocomposite electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 197 (2014) 43-49.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Translation of near-infrared fluorescence imaging technologies: emerging clinical applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.M. Sevickmuraca, Translation of near-infrared fluorescence imaging technologies: emerging clinical applications, Annual Review of Medicine, 63 (2012) 217-231.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots sensor for highly sensitive detection of guanine

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence molecular imaging systems for intraoperative image-guided surgery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.H. Kim, C. Obrien, H.S. Choi, M.Y. Jeong, Fluorescence molecular imaging systems for intraoperative image-guided surgery, Applied Spectroscopy Reviews, 53 (2018) 349-359.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Indocyanine green fluorescence imaging-guided surgery in primary and metastatic liver tumors

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots (C‐dots) from cow manure with impressive subcellular selectivity tuned by simple chemical modification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.D.D.E.S. Barbosa, J.R. Correa, G.A. Medeiros, G. Barreto, K.G. Magalhaes, A.L. De Oliveira, J. Spencer, M.O. Rodrigues, B.A.D. Neto, Carbon dots (C‐dots) from cow manure with impressive subcellular selectivity tuned by simple chemical modification, Chemistry: A European Journal, 21 (2015) 5055-5060.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The visualization of lysosomal and mitochondrial glutathione via near-infrared fluorophore and in vivo imaging application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Xu, M.X. Zhang, Y. Xu, S.H. Liu, L. Zeng, H. Chen, et al., The visualization of lysosomal and mitochondrial glutathione via near-infrared fluorophore and in vivo imaging application, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 290 (2019) 676-683.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescent carbon-dots-based mitochondria-targetable nanoprobe for peroxynitrite sensing in living cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile preparation of bright orange fluorescent carbon dots and the constructed biosensing platform for the detection of pH in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Y. Ding, X.J. Gong, Y. Liu, W.J. Lu, Y.F. Gao, M. Xian, S.M. Shuang, C. Dong, Facile preparation of bright orange fluorescent carbon dots and the constructed biosensing platform for the detection of pH in living cells, Talanta, 189 (2018) 8-15.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Boye Zhang received her M.D. degree in wireless sensor from Taiyuan University of Technology, China, in 2017. Now she is pursuing Ph.D. from MicroNano System Research Center of Taiyuan University of Technology. Her current research focuses on developing multicolored carbon dots for sensing applications.

Qianqian Duan received her Ph.D. degree in material fluorescence characteristics in 2013 from Harbin Institute of Technology, China. She is working in College of Information and Computer of Taiyuan University of Technology as a professor now. Her research mainly focuses on the synthesis, modification, and application of nanoparticles in biomedical and environmental fields.

Haichao Zhao received his M.D. degree in surgery from Shanxi Medical University, China, in 2019. Now he is working in the Department of General Surgery of Shanxi Bethune Hospital and pursuing Ph.D. from Shanxi Medical University. His current research focuses on tumor targeted therapy.

Yixia Zhang received her Ph.D. degree from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, China, in 2014. She now works in the College of Biomedical Engineering of Taiyuan University of Technology, China, mainly engaged in the research of detection techniques and biosensors.

Xiaoning Li received her Ph.D. degree in pathophysiology from Jilin University, China, in 2016. She now works in the Basic Medical College of Shanxi Medical University, China, mainly engaged in the research of molecular mechanisms of cancer development.

Yanfeng Xi received her Ph.D. degree in pathophysiology from Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China, in 2012. She is currently a principal investigator (PI) of Department of Pathology, Shanxi Tumor Hospital. Her research mainly focuses on oncology.

Zhifang Wu received her Ph.D. degree in internal medicine from Shanxi Medical University, China, in 2010. From then on, she is working in the Department of Nuclear Medicine of First Hospital of Shanxi Medical University. Her research mainly focuses on the synthesis and application of probes for medical imaging.

Li Guo received her Ph.D. degree from Shanxi Medical University, China, in 2020. Now she is working in the Department of Orthopedics of the Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University as an attending physician. Her current research focuses on targeted therapy in osteoarthritis.

Pengcui Li received her Ph.D. degree from Shanxi Medical University, China, in 2014. She now works in the Department of Orthopedics, the Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, China, mainly engaged in the research of articular cartilage injury and repair.

Shengbo Sang received his Ph.D. degree in nanoelectromechanical system in 2010 from Ilmenau University of Technology, Germany. He is currently a principal investigator (PI) of MicroNano System Research Center and a full professor of College of Information and Computer, Taiyuan University of Technology, China. His research focuses on the development of advanced electrochemical devices such as chemical sensors and biosensors.
